subheading: Run Alibaba's Qwen-Image model in ComfyUI: text-to-image with strong text rendering, ControlNet, and LoRA support, on Comfy Cloud or your own GPU.
extendedDescription:
If you are evaluating Qwen-Image comfyui workflows, the appeal is an open text-to-image model that handles both pictures and the words inside them. Qwen-Image is Alibaba's open image model from the Qwen family, a 20B-parameter MMDiT released under Apache 2.0, so the weights are open and you can run it on your own hardware rather than renting generations. The workflows here are the ready-made graphs for that: load the model, write a prompt, and generate, with the node graph already wired so you are not building it from scratch.

The model is a solid generalist with a few specialties. It renders text in images more cleanly than most open models, which suits posters and graphics, and it supports structural conditioning through ControlNet so you can drive a composition from an edge map, a pose, or a depth map rather than a prompt alone. It also works with LoRA adapters, so you can pull in a trained style or subject, and the Qwen-Image 2512 refresh sharpens human realism and text layout over the original. There is also a layer-decompose path for separating an image into editable layers, and a Lightning LoRA that cuts a generation down to roughly four steps.

Because these are open workflows, you control the resolution, the step count, the conditioning, and any LoRA you load, and you can re-run until the composition and any text are right. That openness is the difference between a fixed one-shot tool and a graph you can tune for the exact look you are after.

Everything here is open and editable. Open a Qwen-Image workflow on Comfy Cloud with nothing to install, or download a GGUF build and run it locally from around 12GB of VRAM. You can swap conditioning, add a LoRA, adjust the parameters, and there is no watermark on what you generate.

modelSpec.summary: Qwen-Image is Alibaba's open 20B-parameter MMDiT text-to-image model from the Qwen family, Apache 2.0 licensed, with strong text rendering, ControlNet structural conditioning, and LoRA support.
highlights: Open Apache 2.0 weights, a 20B MMDiT runnable locally or on Comfy Cloud
Strong text rendering in images for posters and graphics
Qwen-Image 2512 refresh improves human realism and text layout over the original
ControlNet support to drive composition from edge, pose, or depth maps
Works with LoRA adapters, including a Lightning LoRA for roughly four-step generation
Layer-decompose path for separating an image into editable layers
GGUF builds run from around 12GB of VRAM, with no watermark on output

howToUse: Pick a Qwen-Image workflow: text-to-image, ControlNet, or a LoRA setup.
Open it on Comfy Cloud with no install, or load it in your local ComfyUI.
Write your prompt, including any headline text you want rendered.
Add a control map or a LoRA if the workflow uses one, then set the resolution.
Set the step count, run the workflow, and review the composition and text.
Re-run with adjusted conditioning or parameters, then download the image.

faqItems.question: What are Qwen-Image ComfyUI workflows?
faqItems.answer: They are ready-made ComfyUI graphs that run Qwen-Image, Alibaba's open text-to-image model. Each wires up a task like text-to-image or ControlNet conditioning, so you write a prompt and get a result without building the graph yourself.

faqItems.question: Is Qwen-Image open source?
faqItems.answer: Yes. Qwen-Image ships with open weights from Alibaba's Qwen family, so you can run it on your own hardware. The ComfyUI workflows run it locally or on Comfy Cloud with no setup and no watermark on the output.

faqItems.question: Does Qwen-Image support ControlNet?
faqItems.answer: Yes. The workflows include ControlNet support, so you can drive a composition from an edge map, a pose, or a depth map rather than a prompt alone. That gives you structural control over where things land in the image.

faqItems.question: Can I use LoRA adapters with Qwen-Image?
faqItems.answer: Yes. Qwen-Image works with LoRA adapters, so you can pull in a trained style or subject on top of the base model. Because the graph is open, you set how strongly the LoRA applies and can re-run to tune the result.

faqItems.question: How good is Qwen-Image at rendering text?
faqItems.answer: Qwen-Image renders text in images more cleanly than most open models, with legible, correctly placed lettering. That makes it a reasonable choice for posters, graphics, and other design work where words appear in the frame.

faqItems.question: Can I run Qwen-Image without a powerful GPU?
faqItems.answer: Yes. Run any Qwen-Image workflow on Comfy Cloud in the browser with nothing to install. If you have a capable GPU, download the workflow and run the model locally for full control over every step.

faqItems.question: What VRAM do I need to run Qwen-Image locally?
faqItems.answer: The full BF16 build of the 20B model wants around 40GB, but quantized GGUF builds bring that down a lot. A Q4 build runs in roughly 12GB of VRAM, and lighter quants drop closer to 8GB, so a mainstream card can handle it at some quality cost.

faqItems.question: Do I need to install anything to use these Qwen-Image workflows?
faqItems.answer: Not on Comfy Cloud, which runs the workflow in your browser with no setup and no local download. If you would rather run on your own machine, you install ComfyUI and pull the model and any LoRA you want, then open the same graph locally.

faqItems.question: Does Qwen-Image run locally or only on Comfy Cloud?
faqItems.answer: Both. Because the weights are open under Apache 2.0, you can download a GGUF build and run Qwen-Image on your own GPU. Comfy Cloud is the no-install path when you do not have the hardware, running the identical workflow in the browser.

faqItems.question: How much does it cost to run these workflows on Comfy Cloud?
faqItems.answer: Comfy Cloud is a paid plan with tiered seats, so there is no per-image charge once you are on a plan. Running Qwen-Image locally has no platform fee at all beyond your own hardware and electricity, since the weights are open.
